summary: Stancil argues that most software aims to disappear, but our strongest moments of “delight” come when we feel the machine directly—like scripting, command-line tools, or terminal-based AI. He uses dbt and Claude Code vs. Snowsight to show that stripped-down interfaces can feel more modern and emotionally resonant because they remove intermediation and reveal computational power.
money_quotes: But, up close, there is something mesmerizing about watching a machine do its simple work.
It was our first version of Tom Cruise’s gloves, and our first computational sports car.
Tools like Claude Code puts you as close to the technology as it can.
We are not moved by detachment.
Because real delight—and productivity, if we must—comes from feeling the machine that we live our lives on, rather than forgetting to see that it exists.
key_insight: Software that exposes its machinery—rather than hiding it behind polished UI—can feel more modern, powerful, and emotionally satisfying.
